title: Dog Eyelid Growths: Causes, Urgency, and Veterinary Care
meta_desc: Notice a bump on your dog's eyelid? Learn about common causes, why the urgency is usually low, and how a photo helps your vet provide better care.
content_html:
What is a growth on the eyelid margin?

Finding a bump on your dog's eyelid can be worrying, but these growths are very common, particularly in senior dogs. Most often, a growth on the eyelid margin is a meibomian gland tumor (a sebaceous adenoma), which is typically a benign growth of the oil-producing glands. Other possibilities include styes, cysts, or, less commonly, malignant tumors. Because these growths sit right on the edge of the eyelid, they can sometimes rub against the surface of the eye, causing irritation or even a corneal ulcer.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for a growth on the eyelid margin is Low. In most cases, you do not need to rush to an emergency clinic. However, you should schedule a non-emergency appointment with your veterinarian within the next week to have the mass evaluated. If you notice your dog squinting, pawing at the eye, or if there is significant redness and discharge, the priority increases, as these are signs that the growth is actively damaging the eye itself.

Why a photo helps with triage

When seeking a consultation, providing a clear, well-lit photo of the growth is one of the most helpful things you can do. A high-quality image allows a veterinary professional to see the exact location of the mass, its size, and its texture. This visual information helps determine if the mass is likely to be benign or if it shows concerning characteristics that require more urgent intervention. It also helps the vet assess if the growth is currently touching the cornea, which helps them prioritize your pet's appointment.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter eye drops on my dog's eyelid growth?
faqs.answer: You should never use human eye drops or medications on your dog without a veterinarian's approval, as some ingredients can be harmful or mask symptoms of a more serious issue.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery to remove the growth?
faqs.answer: Many eyelid growths are removed surgically, especially if they are growing quickly or irritating the eye. Small, benign growths that aren't causing discomfort may sometimes be monitored instead.

faqs.question: Are most eyelid bumps in dogs cancerous?
faqs.answer: Fortunately, the majority of eyelid margin tumors in dogs are benign. However, only a veterinarian can confirm this through an exam or a biopsy.
